statement.markdown: Given an N x M matrix where each row is sorted, find the median of the matrix. The matrix can have different sized rows, but each row is guaranteed to be sorted in ascending order. The median is the middle value in a sorted distribution. For a matrix with an odd total number of elements, the median is the middle element. For a matrix with an even total number of elements, the median is the average of the two middle elements.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains two integers N and M, representing the number of rows and columns in the matrix, respectively. Then, for each row, the first line contains the number of elements in that row, followed by the elements themselves.
statement.outputFormat: The median of the matrix as a floating point number.

constraints.description: Number of rows in the matrix.
Maximum number of columns in the matrix.
Number of elements in a row.
Value of an element in the matrix.

examples.explanation: The matrix is [[1, 2], [3, 4]]. The sorted matrix is [1, 2, 3, 4]. The median is (2 + 3) / 2 = 2.5.
The matrix is [[1, 2, 3]]. The sorted matrix is [1, 2, 3]. The median is 2.

hints.text: Consider the matrix as a 1D array by flattening it. Find the median of the flattened array.
Since the matrix is sorted, the median can be found by sorting the array and finding the middle element(s).
If the array length is odd, the median is the element at index (length/2). If the array length is even, calculate the average of the two middle elements.
To find the row length, iterate through each row and count the number of elements.
Flatten the matrix by iterating over the rows and appending each element to a new array.

editorial.title: Finding the Median of a Matrix
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by first flattening the matrix into a single array and then sorting it. The median can then be found based on whether the length of the array is odd or even.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach involves flattening the matrix into a 1D array and then sorting it. This approach is correct but slow, with a time complexity of O(N*M log(N*M)) and a space complexity of O(N*M).
editorial.betterSolution: No better solution exists for this problem as the problem essentially requires sorting the elements of the matrix.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves reading the matrix elements, storing them in an array, sorting the array, and then finding the median. This approach takes advantage of the fact that the median of a sorted array can be found in constant time.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of this approach can be proven by observing that the median of a set of numbers is the middle value when the numbers are sorted in ascending order. If the number of elements is odd, the median is the middle element. If the number of elements is even, the median is the average of the two middle elements.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of this approach with a sample matrix would confirm its correctness.
commonMistakes: Not handling the case where the number of elements is even correctly.
Not handling the case where the input matrix is empty.
Assuming that the matrix is square.
Not using an efficient sorting algorithm.
editorial.timeComplexity: O(T log T) where T is total number of elements (flattening and sorting)
